title: JTBD Analysis — Finfish Farming and Fish Hatcheries (NAICS 112511)

sections.title: Phase 1 — Industry Landscape (Summary)
sections.content:
Phase 1 is pre-computed in `VN_finfish_farming_and_fish_hatcheries.md`. Key parameters carried forward:

| Field | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| **NAICS** | 112511 — Finfish Farming and Fish Hatcheries | VN_finfish_farming |
| **CFJ** | Cultivate finfish from egg or fingerling to market-size harvest in a controlled aquatic environment | VN_finfish_farming |
| **Output Types** | Coldwater RAS (salmon, trout, char); Warmwater RAS (tilapia, barramundi, perch, sturgeon); Hatchery / Fingerling; Flow-Through / Raceway; Pond / Earthen | VN_finfish_farming |
| **Offering Level** | L4 — Component (sensor within monitoring system) | 01_product_decomposition |
| **L6 Domains** | 9 core + 4 horizontal = 13 total | VN_finfish_farming |
| **VN Unit Count** | 162 | VN_finfish_farming |

### 1.2 Segments (Circumstance-Based)

| # | Segment | Circumstance | Alternatives Differ? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Large-scale commercial RAS** (>500 t/yr, salmon focus) | $50M+ capex, 50–200 rearing tanks, full SCADA, 24h staff, biosecurity certification | Yes — demands mag meters or industrial ultrasonic; zero tolerance for measurement gaps |
| 2 | **Mid-scale RAS farm** (50–500 t/yr, tilapia/trout/barramundi) | $2–15M capex, 10–50 tanks, semi-automated PLC, part-time night coverage | Yes — cost-sensitive but high mortality risk; open to ultrasonic upgrade from paddle-wheel |
| 3 | **Hatchery / fingerling producer** | Juvenile stock worth $0.50–5.00/fry, extremely sensitive to DO and temperature swings, small pipe diameters (DN15–DN25) | Yes — precision required but budgets tight; paddle-wheel or no measurement common |
| 4 | **Flow-through / raceway operator** | Continuous water exchange, no recirculation loop, high flow volumes, gravity-fed typical | Yes — flow monitoring optional (water replaces itself); sensor value is intake metering |
| 5 | **Emerging / startup RAS** (<50 t/yr, first-generation facility) | Limited operations expertise, capital-constrained, often university spin-out or investor-backed | Yes — needs lowest total cost of ownership; paddle-wheel or manual observation dominant |

sections.title: Phase 2 — Company Enters
sections.content:
### 2.1 VN Anchor

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Anchor Route** | PROCESS_VN — product is equipment used to MAKE the output (fish), not part of the output |
| **Anchor Level** | L4 — Component |
| **Anchor Unit** | L4.10.1.1 Ultrasonic Flow Sensor |
| **Parent VN Unit** | L5.10.1 Flow Measurement |
| **Parent Level** | L5 |
| **Grandparent** | L6.10 Water Quality Monitoring & Control (H) |
| **L-Path** | L7 Finfish Aquaculture Ecosystem > L6.10 Water Quality Monitoring & Control > L5.10.1 Flow Measurement > L4.10.1.1 Ultrasonic Flow Sensor |

**Secondary anchors:** L5.6.1 Heat Exchanger monitoring loop (Temperature Control), L5.1.1 Source Water Pump Station (Water Intake flow metering)

### 2.2 Lens Formation

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Route** | Maker — PROCESS_VN |
| **Lens Source** | Process VN node job_family |
| **Function Verb** | Monitor and quantify (from L5.10.1 Flow Measurement) |
| **Lens** | "What monitoring and measurement does an aquaculture operations team need in the water circulation system?" |

**Segment filtering:**

| Segment | VN Node Serves? | Product Modality Serves? |
|---|---|---|
| Large-scale commercial RAS | Yes | Partially (DN20/DN25 covers tank-level feeds; main loop >DN50 requires clamp-on or mag) |
| Mid-scale RAS farm | Yes | Yes (primary target — DN20/DN25 matches typical RAS piping; cost and no-clogging advantage) |
| Hatchery / fingerling producer | Yes | Yes (DN14/DN20 matches small nursery piping; low-energy mode for battery backup) |
| Flow-through / raceway | Yes | Partially (intake metering only; no recirculation loop) |
| Emerging / startup RAS | Yes | Yes (lowest maintenance burden; cost-competitive with paddle-wheel over 2-year TCO) |

### 2.3 Product Job

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Product Job Statement** | Help Flow Measurement to quantify water circulation rate across rearing and treatment loops in recirculating aquaculture systems without obstructing flow or clogging from biofilm |
| **Parent Unit** | Flow Measurement (L5) |
| **Context Qualifier** | In recirculating aquaculture systems |
| **Abstraction Test** | 5 alternatives from 4 different categories (ultrasonic, electromagnetic, paddle-wheel/turbine, differential-pressure, do nothing) — PASS as Core Job |

### 2.4 Alternatives (5)

| # | Alternative | UNSPSC | Inherent Trade-Offs |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ultrasonic transit-time flow sensor** | 41112501 | ±1–3% accuracy (vs ±0.5% mag); degrades with air bubbles >2% vol; requires 5–10×DN straight pipe run; needs fluid-type configuration at commissioning; emerging awareness in aquaculture [SRC: web_search] |
| 2 | **Electromagnetic (mag) flowmeter** | 41112502 | 5–10× cost ($500–2,000+ vs $100–300 for inline ultrasonic); higher accuracy (±0.5%) but overkill for most farm-scale monitoring; larger footprint; higher power consumption (10–20 W); requires conductive liquid (met by default in freshwater RAS) [SRC: web_search] |
| 3 | **Paddle-wheel / turbine flow sensor** | 41112501 | Very low cost ($20–80); BUT clogs with biofilm in 2–6 weeks in RAS water; moving parts wear in particulate-laden water; contamination risk from bearing lubricant; no anomaly detection; ±2–5% accuracy drift; 45–55% incumbent share [SRC: VN_finfish_farming, web_search] |
| 4 | **Differential-pressure measurement** (orifice/venturi) | 41113201 | Permanent pressure drop wastes pump energy (critical in energy-intensive RAS); fouling of sensing ports by biofilm; limited turndown ratio (3:1); requires DP transmitter; rarely used in aquaculture [SRC: web_search] |
| 5 | **Do nothing / manual observation** [Status Quo] | — | Zero cost — 15–25% of farms; completely blind to flow failure; relies on downstream DO alarm or fish behavioral change; by the time operators notice, mortality may already be underway; historically acceptable on small ponds, catastrophic in RAS [SRC: VN_finfish_farming] |

### 2.5 Scenarios and Output Type Activation

| Scenario | Output Types Activated | VN Position Engaged |
|---|---|---|
| **Coldwater RAS production** (salmon/trout, 10–16°C, 40–100 kg/m³) | Coldwater RAS | ALL L6 steps — maximum equipment demand; flow monitoring life-critical |
| **Warmwater RAS production** (tilapia/barramundi, 26–30°C, 30–80 kg/m³) | Warmwater RAS | ALL L6 steps; higher DO consumption per kg biomass at elevated temp |
| **Hatchery/fry rearing** (eggs to fingerlings, <50 g) | Hatchery / Fingerling | L6.1–L6.7 + L6.10–L6.13; smaller pipe diameters; extreme DO sensitivity |
| **Flow-through/raceway** (continuous water exchange) | Flow-Through / Raceway | L6.1 + L6.7–L6.9 + partial L6.10; intake flow metering only; no recirculation |
| **Heat exchanger loop monitoring** (secondary position) | Coldwater RAS, Warmwater RAS, Hatchery | L6.6 Temperature Control; verify flow through chiller/heater circuits |

**Sparsity check:** RAS scenarios activate ALL L6 steps; flow-through skips mechanical/biological filtration and recirculation monitoring. Hatchery activates all but with smaller pipe sizes. Not every scenario activates every position — PASS.

---

sections.title: Phase 3 — Needs Derivation
sections.content:
### 3.1 Stakeholder Roles

| Role | Who in Aquaculture Context | Pyramid Levels |
|---|---|---|
| **Job Executor** | RAS operations technician / farm manager | P1 ALL, P2 ALL, P3, P4, P5 |
| **Job Overseer** | Production manager / facility owner | P1 own, P2 own, P4, P3* |
| **Job Beneficiary** | The fish (biological outcome) / end consumer (food safety, indirect) | P1*, P2*, P5 |
| **Product Lifecycle Support** | Maintenance technician / system integrator | P1 own (install, calibrate, service) |
| **Purchase Executor** | Farm owner / equipment procurement manager | P1 Acquisition |
| **Purchase Influencer** | RAS system OEM (AKVA, AquaMaof, Innovasea) / aquaculture consultant / veterinarian | P2 own, P4 |

### 3.2 Job Map (8 Ulwick Steps)

| # | Step | Statement | Relevant? | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DEFINE** | Define the required water flow rate per rearing tank and treatment loop based on stocking density, species, and water treatment capacity | **Yes** | Sensor range must match the required flow window; over/under-specification has biological consequences |
| 2 | LOCATE | Locate and access the correct pipe section for sensor installation in the water treatment loop | No | Physical pipe routing is independent of sensor technology |
| 3 | **PREPARE** | Install, commission, and configure the flow sensor for the specific water conditions (freshwater, brackish, or recirculated) | **Yes** | Commissioning procedure and medium configuration are sensor-dependent |
| 4 | **CONFIRM** | Verify the sensor is reading within specified accuracy before stocking fish or after system maintenance | **Yes** | Pre-stocking verification is critical; flow sensor failure after stocking = mortality risk |
| 5 | **EXECUTE** | Measure water circulation rate continuously across all rearing tanks and treatment stages 24/7/365 | **Yes** | Core measurement function — the primary value-creating and LIFE-SAFETY action in RAS |
| 6 | **MONITOR** | Monitor flow readings for drift, anomalies, pump degradation, or pipe blockage; trigger alarms on flow failure | **Yes** | Flow failure alarm is the most critical safety trigger in RAS — DO crash follows within minutes |
| 7 | **MODIFY** | Adjust water distribution, redirect flow, or troubleshoot when flow readings deviate from expected values | **Yes** | Sensor data quality determines troubleshooting speed in a life-threatening situation |
| 8 | CONCLUDE | Log flow data for production records, regulatory compliance, and water budget tracking | No | Data logging depends on SCADA/PLC, not sensor |

**Relevant steps: 6 of 8** (DEFINE, PREPARE, CONFIRM, EXECUTE, MONITOR, MODIFY)

### 3.3 P1 + P2 Error Statements (per Job Step, 7 Error Types)

### Burleson Pyramid — P1 Product Jobs by Alternative

```

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

(Marquardt)       order variant,      freshwater;       output 24/7;      maintenance;      unplug;
                  coordinate with     verify straight   set flow-fail     wipe exterior     no hazardous
                  RAS OEM             pipe run          alarm threshold   annually          waste

Mag flowmeter     Specify pipe size,  Wire 24V power,   Read 4-20mA or   Clean electrodes  Disconnect
                  budget 5-10× cost,  ground properly,  Modbus; highest   if biofilm builds power and
                  plan power supply   zero-calibrate    accuracy          on tube lining    signal wiring

Paddle-wheel      Select from         Thread into pipe, Visual or pulse   Replace rotor/    Unthread;
                  catalog, cheapest   verify flow       output; manual    bearings every    dispose worn
                                      direction         check rotation    2-6 weeks in RAS  bearings

Diff-pressure     Size orifice,       Install manifold, Read DP           Flush sensing     Remove orifice
                  specify DP range    fill impulse      transmitter;      ports weekly;     plate and
                                      lines, zero       sqrt extraction   clear biofilm     manifold

Do nothing        None                None              Visual check of   None              None
                                                        fish behavior;
                                                        hope for the best
```

### Error Statements (25 total)

#### Step 5 — EXECUTE (Core Measurement — Life-Safety)

| # | Error Statement | Job Step | Error Type | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | product_related | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Minimize the likelihood of undetected flow cessation in a rearing tank supply line persisting longer than 60 seconds before alarm activation | EXECUTE | DETECTION | 10 | 3 | 17 | safety | false | 0.9 |
| 2 | Minimize the likelihood of undetected calibration drift exceeding ±1% of reading over a 12-month continuous operation period in biofilm-prone water | EXECUTE | ACCURACY | 8 | 3 | 13 | reliability | false | 0.7 |
| 3 | Minimize variation in flow measurement accuracy caused by seasonal water temperature fluctuations between 2°C and 30°C across species programs | EXECUTE | CONSISTENCY | 7 | 4 | 10 | reliability | false | 0.7 |
| 4 | Increase the ability to measure flow rate across the full operating range (2–60 L/min per tank feed) without sensor reconfiguration between tank sizes | EXECUTE | THROUGHPUT | 7 | 5 | 9 | reliability | true | 0.7 |

#### Step 6 — MONITOR (Fault Detection — Life-Safety)

| # | Error Statement | Job Step | Error Type | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | product_related | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | Minimize the time to detect a circulation pump degradation event that reduces tank water exchange rate by >20% before dissolved oxygen drops below 6 mg/L | MONITOR | DETECTION | 10 | 2 | 18 | safety | false | 0.9 |
| 6 | Minimize the number of undetected pipe blockage events (biofilm growth, scale accumulation) that restrict flow by >15% in treatment loops | MONITOR | DETECTION | 9 | 3 | 15 | reliability | false | 0.8 |
| 7 | Increase the ability to distinguish genuine flow anomalies (pump cavitation, valve failure) from sensor measurement artifacts with >95% classification accuracy | MONITOR | ACCURACY | 8 | 3 | 13 | reliability | false | 0.7 |
| 8 | Minimize the likelihood of false flow-normal readings during air-lock events in recirculation piping that mask actual flow cessation | MONITOR | ROBUSTNESS | 9 | 3 | 15 | safety | true | 0.8 |

#### Step 1 — DEFINE

| # | Error Statement | Job Step | Error Type | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | product_related | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 9 | Increase the ability to determine the correct flow rate setpoint within ±10% of actual biological oxygen demand for each species, stocking density, and growth stage | DEFINE | ACCURACY | 8 | 3 | 13 | safety | false | 0.7 |
| 10 | Minimize the effort required to calculate per-tank flow requirements when stocking density changes during grow-out cycles | DEFINE | EFFORT | 6 | 4 | 8 | skill | false | 0.5 |

#### Step 3 — PREPARE (Installation & Commissioning)

| # | Error Statement | Job Step | Error Type | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | product_related | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 11 | Minimize the time required to install and commission a flow sensor on an existing RAS pipe loop without draining the circuit or interrupting fish production | PREPARE | EFFORT | 8 | 3 | 13 | time | true | 0.8 |
| 12 | Minimize the likelihood of systematic measurement error >1% caused by incorrect medium configuration at commissioning for recirculated water with varying dissolved solids | PREPARE | ACCURACY | 7 | 4 | 10 | reliability | true | 0.6 |

#### Step 4 — CONFIRM (Pre-Stocking Verification)

| # | Error Statement | Job Step | Error Type | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | product_related | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 13 | Increase the ability to verify sensor accuracy on-site within 10 minutes before stocking fish into a newly prepared tank | CONFIRM | DETECTION | 8 | 3 | 13 | time | false | 0.7 |
| 14 | Minimize the likelihood of accepting a false-normal flow reading after system maintenance that masks a partially closed valve or misrouted pipe | CONFIRM | DETECTION | 9 | 3 | 15 | safety | false | 0.8 |

#### Step 7 — MODIFY (Troubleshooting — Time-Critical)

| # | Error Statement | Job Step | Error Type | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | product_related | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 15 | Increase the ability to isolate flow anomaly root cause (sensor fault vs pump failure vs valve position vs pipe blockage) within 3 minutes during an active alarm | MODIFY | RECOVERY | 9 | 2 | 16 | time | false | 0.8 |
| 16 | Minimize the operator expertise required to interpret flow alarm data and determine the correct corrective action during a night shift with minimal staffing | MODIFY | EFFORT | 8 | 3 | 13 | skill | false | 0.7 |

#### P1 Lifecycle — Technology-Specific (per Alternative)

| # | Error Statement | Lifecycle Stage | Error Type | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | product_related | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 17 | Increase the sensor service life achievable without scheduled maintenance interventions in biofilm-prone recirculated water (target: >5 years with zero clogging events) | MAINTENANCE | EFFORT | 9 | 2 | 16 | cost | true | 0.9 |
| 18 | Minimize the total installed cost per water flow measurement point including sensor, wiring, and integration with existing PLC/SCADA (target: <$400) | ACQUISITION | EFFORT | 8 | 4 | 12 | cost | true | 0.8 |
| 19 | Minimize the likelihood of sensor-induced flow path contamination or turbulence that affects dissolved oxygen transfer downstream of the measurement point | USAGE | ROBUSTNESS | 7 | 5 | 9 | safety | true | 0.7 |
| 20 | Minimize the permanent pressure drop across the measurement point that increases circulation pump energy consumption in an energy-intensive RAS facility | USAGE | THROUGHPUT | 7 | 5 | 9 | cost | true | 0.7 |

#### P2 — Core Job (Solution-Independent)

| # | Error Statement | Error Type | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | product_related | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 21 | Minimize the likelihood of a total stock loss event (>10% biomass mortality) caused by undetected water circulation failure in any single rearing unit | ROBUSTNESS | 10 | 3 | 17 | safety | false | 0.9 |
| 22 | Increase the ability to maintain water exchange rate within ±10% of setpoint across all rearing tanks simultaneously during peak biomass loading | CONSISTENCY | 8 | 4 | 12 | reliability | false | 0.7 |

### 3.4 P3–P5 Bridge Needs

#### P3 — Role Identity (Executor: RAS Operations Technician / Overseer: Production Manager)

| Retrieval | Bridge | Need |
|---|---|---|
| "By getting water circulation monitoring done well, the RAS technician becomes the person who guarantees fish welfare and production uptime — the operator who has never lost a tank" | "What must the product do so they CAN become that role?" | See #23 |

| # | Error Statement | Level | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | product_related | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 23 | Increase the ability to present quantitative water circulation performance data to facility investors, insurers, and certification auditors as evidence of operational control | P3 Role | 7 | 3 | 11 | skill | false | 0.5 |

#### P5 — Emotional (Executor: Night-Shift Technician / Farm Manager)

| Retrieval | Bridge | Need |
|---|---|---|
| "They want to feel confident that fish are safe overnight when staffing is minimal. They want to avoid the dread of arriving to a mass mortality event — hundreds of thousands of dead fish and months of production lost — because a pump failed at 2 AM and nobody noticed" | "What must the product do so they FEEL the wanted state?" | See #24, #25 |

| # | Error Statement | Level | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | product_related | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 24 | Minimize the likelihood of discovering a mass mortality event caused by overnight flow failure that was not detected by the monitoring system [hypothesis] | P5 Emotional | 10 | 3 | 17 | safety | false | 0.6 |
| 25 | Minimize the anxiety associated with leaving a high-value RAS facility ($500K+ biomass at risk) unattended during off-hours by providing reliable remote flow-failure alerts [hypothesis] | P5 Emotional | 8 | 3 | 13 | reliability | false | 0.5 |

sections.title: Validation (QA Pass)
sections.content:

| V2 | Product-related classification | **PASS** | product_related=true only where a different technology would eliminate the need (e.g., #8 air-lock masking = ultrasonic-specific; #20 pressure drop = orifice-specific; #17 clogging = paddle-wheel-specific) |

| V5 | Coverage | **PASS** | ≥2 needs per relevant step; 68% market-inherent, 32% technology-specific; no duplicates |
| V6 | Solution-agnostic | **PASS** | Zero references to "Marquardt," "ultrasonic," "piezoelectric," or any specific product in need statements |
| V7 | Anchor correctness | **PASS** | L4 anchor matches component abstraction level |
| V8 | Product job references parent | **PASS** | "Help Flow Measurement to..." includes parent VN unit name and context qualifier |

sections.title: Cost-of-Failure Context
sections.content:
| Failure Mode | Estimated Impact | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Total flow cessation in RAS rearing tank | DO crashes below 3 mg/L within 5–15 minutes at high stocking density; 100% mortality in affected tank within 30–60 min | [SRC: web_search — Wiley, Frontiers Marine Science] |
| Pump degradation undetected (gradual) | Reduced water exchange → rising ammonia/nitrite → chronic stress → disease outbreak → 10–40% mortality over days | [SRC: web_search — ScienceDirect, Springer] |
| Atlantic Sapphire RAS failures (case study) | 500,000 salmon killed in single incident; $51.5M net loss in H1 2021; $250M market cap loss; 4+ mass mortality events | [SRC: web_search — SeafoodSource, The Fish Site, Alan W Cook Consulting] |
| Biomass at risk per tank (commercial RAS) | 40–100 kg/m³ × 200–500 m³ tank = 8–50 tonnes per tank; market value $4–12/kg = $30K–600K per tank | [SRC: web_search — FAO, RASTECH] |
| Paddle-wheel sensor biofilm clogging | Rotor jams within 2–6 weeks in RAS water; false-normal readings during progressive clogging; requires manual inspection cycle | [SRC: web_search — ICON Process Controls, pflowmeters.com] |

---

"What monitoring and measurement does an aquaculture operations team need in the water circulation system?"

Yes (primary target — DN20/DN25 matches typical RAS piping; cost and no-clogging advantage)

Partially (intake metering only; no recirculation loop)

rows.Value: Help Flow Measurement to quantify water circulation rate across rearing and treatment loops in recirculating aquaculture systems without obstructing flow or clogging from biofilm
5 alternatives from 4 different categories (ultrasonic, electromagnetic, paddle-wheel/turbine, differential-pressure, do nothing) — PASS as Core Job

rows.Inherent Trade-Offs: ±1–3% accuracy (vs ±0.5% mag); degrades with air bubbles >2% vol; requires 5–10×DN straight pipe run; needs fluid-type configuration at commissioning; emerging awareness in aquaculture [SRC: web_search]

5–10× cost ($500–2,000+ vs $100–300 for inline ultrasonic); higher accuracy (±0.5%) but overkill for most farm-scale monitoring; larger footprint; higher power consumption (10–20 W); requires conductive liquid (met by default in freshwater RAS) [SRC: web_search]

Very low cost ($20–80); BUT clogs with biofilm in 2–6 weeks in RAS water; moving parts wear in particulate-laden water; contamination risk from bearing lubricant; no anomaly detection; ±2–5% accuracy drift; 45–55% incumbent share [SRC: VN_finfish_farming, web_search]

Permanent pressure drop wastes pump energy (critical in energy-intensive RAS); fouling of sensing ports by biofilm; limited turndown ratio (3:1); requires DP transmitter; rarely used in aquaculture [SRC: web_search]

Zero cost — 15–25% of farms; completely blind to flow failure; relies on downstream DO alarm or fish behavioral change; by the time operators notice, mortality may already be underway; historically acceptable on small ponds, catastrophic in RAS [SRC: VN_finfish_farming]

rows.Rationale: Physical pipe routing is independent of sensor technology

rows.Statement: Install, commission, and configure the flow sensor for the specific water conditions (freshwater, brackish, or recirculated)

alternatives.name: Ultrasonic transit-time flow sensor
alternatives.trade_offs: ±1–3% accuracy; degrades with air bubbles >2% vol; requires 5–10×DN straight pipe run; needs fluid-type configuration; emerging awareness in aquaculture

alternatives.name: Electromagnetic (mag) flowmeter
alternatives.trade_offs: 5–10× cost ($500–2,000+); higher accuracy but overkill; larger footprint; higher power (10–20 W); requires conductive liquid

alternatives.name: Paddle-wheel / turbine flow sensor
alternatives.trade_offs: Very low cost ($20–80); clogs with biofilm in 2–6 weeks in RAS; moving parts wear; contamination risk; ±2–5% accuracy drift; 45–55% incumbent share

alternatives.name: Differential-pressure measurement (orifice/venturi)
alternatives.trade_offs: Permanent pressure drop; fouling of sensing ports by biofilm; limited turndown (3:1); rarely used in aquaculture

alternatives.name: Do nothing / manual observation
alternatives.trade_offs: Zero cost — 15–25% of farms; blind to flow failure; relies on fish behavior or downstream DO alarm; historically acceptable on small ponds, catastrophic in RAS

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of undetected flow cessation in a rearing tank supply line persisting longer than 60 seconds before alarm activation
error_statements.rationale: Flow cessation in RAS causes DO crash below 3 mg/L within 5–15 minutes at commercial stocking density; 100% mortality in affected tank possible within 30–60 minutes

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of undetected calibration drift exceeding ±1% of reading over a 12-month continuous operation period in biofilm-prone water
error_statements.rationale: Uncorrected drift in biofilm-laden RAS water accumulates over months, distorting flow setpoints and degrading dissolved oxygen delivery without triggering alarms

error_statements.statement: Minimize variation in flow measurement accuracy caused by seasonal water temperature fluctuations between 2°C and 30°C across species programs
error_statements.rationale: Coldwater salmonids at 10–16°C and warmwater tilapia at 26–30°C operate on the same facility; speed-of-sound shift across this range introduces systematic error if uncompensated

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to measure flow rate across the full operating range (2–60 L/min per tank feed) without sensor reconfiguration between tank sizes
error_statements.rationale: Tank sizes range from 1 m³ nursery to 500 m³ grow-out; individual feed pipes vary from DN14 to DN25; sensor must cover full range with one configuration

error_statements.statement: Minimize the time to detect a circulation pump degradation event that reduces tank water exchange rate by >20% before dissolved oxygen drops below 6 mg/L
error_statements.rationale: Gradual pump impeller wear or VFD degradation reduces flow slowly; by the time DO sensors alarm, multiple tanks may already be oxygen-stressed; direct flow measurement detects degradation earlier than indirect DO response

error_statements.statement: Minimize the number of undetected pipe blockage events (biofilm growth, scale accumulation) that restrict flow by >15% in treatment loops
error_statements.rationale: Biofilm accumulation in RAS piping is continuous; progressive restriction reduces treatment capacity (UV dose, biofiltration, oxygenation) before total blockage alarm

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to distinguish genuine flow anomalies (pump cavitation, valve failure) from sensor measurement artifacts with >95% classification accuracy
error_statements.rationale: False alarms at 2 AM erode operator trust and cause alarm fatigue; missed alarms risk mass mortality; signal-to-noise must support reliable 24/7 automated alarming

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of false flow-normal readings during air-lock events in recirculation piping that mask actual flow cessation
error_statements.rationale: Air locks in RAS piping can occur after maintenance, pump cavitation, or degasser malfunction; some sensor types continue reading 'normal' during partial air lock while actual water flow to fish is interrupted

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to determine the correct flow rate setpoint within ±10% of actual biological oxygen demand for each species, stocking density, and growth stage
error_statements.rationale: Over-specification wastes pump energy; under-specification causes DO deficit under peak feeding; most RAS operators lack engineering data for flow sizing per species and biomass loading

error_statements.statement: Minimize the effort required to calculate per-tank flow requirements when stocking density changes during grow-out cycles
error_statements.rationale: Biomass increases 50–100× from fingerling to harvest; flow setpoints must track growth curve; recalculation is manual and error-prone for operators without aquaculture engineering training

error_statements.statement: Minimize the time required to install and commission a flow sensor on an existing RAS pipe loop without draining the circuit or interrupting fish production
error_statements.rationale: Draining RAS loops interrupts biological filtration and stresses fish; inline sensor installation during live production requires fast mechanical connection and minimal pipe modification

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of systematic measurement error >1% caused by incorrect medium configuration at commissioning for recirculated water with varying dissolved solids
error_statements.rationale: RAS water chemistry differs from clean municipal water due to dissolved organic compounds, ammonia, and mineral buildup; sensor compensation algorithms must account for these differences

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to verify sensor accuracy on-site within 10 minutes before stocking fish into a newly prepared tank
error_statements.rationale: Stocking fish commits $30K–600K of biomass to the monitored circuit; pre-stocking flow verification is the last safety check before live animals are at risk

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of accepting a false-normal flow reading after system maintenance that masks a partially closed valve or misrouted pipe
error_statements.rationale: Post-maintenance valve misposition is a documented cause of RAS mortality events (Atlantic Sapphire Denmark 2021 — human error altered water levels and allowed stagnant water to reach fish tanks)

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to isolate flow anomaly root cause (sensor fault vs pump failure vs valve position vs pipe blockage) within 3 minutes during an active alarm
error_statements.rationale: In RAS, every minute of flow interruption reduces DO; a 3-minute diagnosis window is the maximum before emergency oxygen injection becomes necessary; multiple possible failure modes require rapid differentiation

error_statements.statement: Minimize the operator expertise required to interpret flow alarm data and determine the correct corrective action during a night shift with minimal staffing
error_statements.rationale: Night-shift operators are often less experienced; alarm data must be immediately interpretable without domain expertise; ambiguous alarms lead to delayed response or incorrect intervention

error_statements.statement: Increase the sensor service life achievable without scheduled maintenance interventions in biofilm-prone recirculated water (target: >5 years with zero clogging events)
error_statements.rationale: Paddle-wheel incumbent clogs in 2–6 weeks in RAS water; electromagnetic meters resist clogging but cost 5–10×; ultrasonic no-moving-parts design eliminates biofilm jamming — the core differentiator in aquaculture

error_statements.statement: Minimize the total installed cost per water flow measurement point including sensor, wiring, and integration with existing PLC/SCADA (target: <$400)
error_statements.rationale: A 50-tank RAS needs 50–100 flow measurement points; at mag-meter cost ($500–2,000 each) total investment is prohibitive; aquaculture operators are more price-sensitive than industrial process customers

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of sensor-induced flow path contamination or turbulence that affects dissolved oxygen transfer downstream of the measurement point
error_statements.rationale: Any obstruction in the flow path creates turbulence and micro-bubble formation which can affect downstream DO transfer and gas management efficiency

error_statements.statement: Minimize the permanent pressure drop across the measurement point that increases circulation pump energy consumption in an energy-intensive RAS facility
error_statements.rationale: Energy is the second-largest RAS operating cost after feed; circulation pumps run 24/7; every mbar of added pressure drop across 50–100 measurement points compounds into significant annual energy cost

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of a total stock loss event (>10% biomass mortality) caused by undetected water circulation failure in any single rearing unit
error_statements.rationale: Atlantic Sapphire lost 500,000 salmon in a single event from equipment/design failure; biomass value per tank can reach $600K; flow monitoring is the earliest detection layer before DO and ammonia alarms

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to maintain water exchange rate within ±10% of setpoint across all rearing tanks simultaneously during peak biomass loading
error_statements.rationale: At peak stocking density (80–100 kg/m³) biological oxygen demand is highest; uneven flow distribution means some tanks are under-served while others are over-served; per-tank flow measurement enables rebalancing

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to present quantitative water circulation performance data to facility investors, insurers, and certification auditors as evidence of operational control
error_statements.rationale: RAS facilities are capital-intensive; investors and aquaculture insurers require evidence of monitoring and alarm capability; ASC/BAP certification increasingly demands sensor-based water quality documentation

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of discovering a mass mortality event caused by overnight flow failure that was not detected by the monitoring system
error_statements.rationale: Arriving to a tank of dead fish is the worst outcome in aquaculture operations; the emotional and financial trauma drives operators to demand reliable 24/7 alarm systems; flow monitoring is the earliest indicator of failure

error_statements.statement: Minimize the anxiety associated with leaving a high-value RAS facility ($500K+ biomass at risk) unattended during off-hours by providing reliable remote flow-failure alerts
error_statements.rationale: Many mid-scale RAS farms lack 24h staff; operators rely on SMS/email alerts from SCADA; confidence in alert reliability determines whether the owner can sleep at night or stays anxious about $500K of live biomass

incumbent_anchors.rationale: Incumbent technology in 45–55% of RAS installations; very low cost ($20–80) but clogs with biofilm in 2–6 weeks; moving parts wear in particulate-laden water
Established in 10–20% of large commercial RAS; high accuracy (±0.5%); no clogging; but 5–10× cost makes it prohibitive for per-tank deployment in mid-scale farms
Legacy approach in 15–25% of smaller farms and pond operations; zero cost but completely blind to flow failure; relies on downstream DO alarm or fish behavioral change
